metric_name: Wall Street behavior: earnings estimate inflation
metric_value: Earnings estimates scaled up by pennies/nickels/dimes to justify lofty P/E multiples when numerator (stock price) in runaway bull market
notes: Cannot change numerator in runaway market; only lever is EPS denominator

metric_name: March quarter seasonality factor 1: customer budget timing
metric_value: Major customers on calendar year; Q1 is breather after year-end close. January nullity; February signs of life; March bookings hopefully strong.
notes: Structural factor 1 of 4 explaining March quarter softness

metric_name: March quarter seasonality factor 2: new budget caution
metric_value: New spending budgets not turned on full force in Q1; period of reflection and re-examination; 'squirrel away a penny or two' mentality
notes: Structural factor 2 of 4; particularly pronounced after tough prior year

metric_name: March quarter seasonality factor 3: institutional behavior
metric_value: Informal institutionalization of modest March quarter; annual sales conferences, vacations for top performers (who bring 70-80% of business); planning conferences
notes: Structural factor 3 of 4; 80-20 rule: top reps bring 70-80% of business

metric_name: March quarter seasonality factor 4: psychological reset
metric_value: New Year's Resolution effect: buyers and sellers urge to 'think it through' rather than leap onto quarterly treadmill to keep analysts at bay
notes: Structural factor 4 of 4; psychological rather than operational

metric_name: Severity of 1981-1982 recession
metric_value: Worst recession in fifty years; stretched all companies to limit and some beyond
notes: Context: 'The Recent Dismalness'; referenced alongside Office Automation Outlook

metric_name: High tech sector recession response
metric_value: High Tech America did outstanding job: hunkered down, invested for future (depressing near-term margins), prepared for recovery
notes: Led some analysts to call tech firms 'recession proof or recession resistant'

metric_name: Investor logic error: post-recession snap-back expectation
metric_value: Logic: if tech firms did well in recession, recovery will bring massive sales increases and margin expansion. Casale calls this investor logic 'inevitible'
notes: Casale argues real world not so kind; recent March quarter results proving this wrong

metric_name: Margin bounceback barrier 1: deferred maintenance/investment
metric_value: During deep recession firms cut discretionary spend then hit bone; strong pressure to restore investments before competitors gain ground keeps margins low
notes: First of four factors preventing instant margin expansion

metric_name: Margin bounceback barrier 2: need for 20%+ revenue growth
metric_value: 20%+ revenue growth is minimum for high-tech group; a year's incremental gains can be squeezed from existing headcount but penalizes future; new hires cost money
notes: Second of four factors; good managements work for long term via incentive stock options

metric_name: Margin bounceback barrier 3: headcount and career progression
metric_value: Career plans in tech assume onward/upward progression; static headcount hurts morale; new hires and support cost money
notes: Third of four factors preventing margin expansion

metric_name: Margin bounceback barrier 4: competitive parity in depression
metric_value: If all competitors in same depressed earnings boat, rational to do right for long term; invest now as long as enough business covers costs and modest margins
notes: Fourth of four factors; fine line between investing for future and vanishing profits

metric_name: Earnings cord-snapping incidents
metric_value: A few companies have snapped too-tightly-stretched cord pursuing quarterly earnings needed to justify lofty multiples; stock prices punished for missing supposedly discounted calendar 1983
notes: Specific company names not given; 'some recent March quarter preliminary results'

metric_name: Short-termism of investors
metric_value: Stock punished for missing March quarter that was 'supposedly discounted' into price; confirms industry's comments about short-term nature of investors
notes: Casale: 'Are managements collectively correct in trying every trick in the book?'

metric_name: Longer-term outlook for high-tech sector
metric_value: Casale positive about longer-term prospects for high tech sector overall
notes: Despite near-term pain, structural optimism maintained

metric_name: Longer-term outlook for high-tech sector
metric_value: [UNVERIFIED]

metric_name: Near-term tech stock correction from over-expectation
metric_value: Raging bull leg of market has led many to expect too much too soon from too many; earnings disappointments and P/E compression likely
notes: Primary thesis of EVP 7; written April 1983 as March quarter results arriving

metric_name: Near-term tech stock correction from over-expectation
metric_value: [UNVERIFIED]

metric_name: P/E compression mechanism in bull market
metric_value: Can only manipulate denominator (EPS) when numerator (price) is running away; leads to artificially inflated earnings estimates
notes: Casale describes mechanism as 'force' (per force): analysts had no choice

metric_name: Sales force concentration (80-20 rule)
metric_value: Top performers bring 70-80% of business in tech companies
notes: Referenced in context of sales conference scheduling reducing March quarter activity

metric_name: Minimum revenue growth threshold for high-tech group
metric_value: 20%+ revenue growth is stated minimum requirement for high-tech group membership
notes: Used to argue why margin expansion requires ongoing investment, not instant recovery
